feedback.text: Hello, I am sending you this email because I am a member of the Europenne initiative ‘Stop killing games’, which ends on 31/07/2025. This initiative (having passed the 1435000 signatures) aims to raise awareness of the disappearance of legitimately purchased video games, which become unplayable when publishers decide to withdraw them from the market or disable their servers. JAI learned that the Digital Fairness Act is being devised and could represent an opportunity to defend consumer rights in the digital field. I would like to suggest a specific strand concerning video games removed or rendered unplayable by publishers. Simply what “top killing game” seeks to do is to have this practice examined so that clear and precise rules are laid down. There is currently no clear law covering the right of a conssumer to buy a video game, leaving publishers (Ubisoft, EA, etc.) the choice to engage in anti-consumer actions. The biggest actions being: remove access to a video game purchased by a customer for any reason. The main reason is that they have to delete their servers because they are no longer profitable. A point on these servers, they are not the time squeeze and a large part of them concern only minor parts of these games which are linked to a point you put forward: addictive designs (in loccurence loot box, gambling). For the rest of what these publishers purport to provide on these servers are time plugs of matchmaking or security services. In general, there are passionate players and developers who revive these services, but unfortunately the non-regularisation of video game sales practices allow publishers to kill these rescue attempts at source by adding important data when to the proper functioning of games on the so-called server (information that should be available locally on consumers’ machines), making it impossible to save the games in question and forcing consumers to turn to other or new products of the same publishers. This type of practice seriously undermines digital ownership and the preservation of video cultural heritage. It also penalises honest buyers, who find themselves with a product that they can no longer use without recourse. You can find a list of several hundred games that have already been deleted or are at risk, by searching for Dead games list. I hope my message will get your attention. Thomas Guyonvarch
